text:
$SIVE is being mispriced because the market is still reading it as a small photonics hardware vendor.

That framing is outdated.

The real position is inside the AI optical stack, at a specific constraint layer: the laser source.

AI networking stack is shifting fast:

GPU clusters
→ scale-out bandwidth demand
→ 800G to 1.6T optics transition
→ CPO / LPO architecture shift
→ laser source layer becomes the constraint
→ InP / EML / CW laser ecosystem

Sivers sits in that lower layer.

Not as a module assembler.

But closer to the optical source and integration bottleneck that determines whether higher-level optics can actually scale.

The key shift the market is missing:

AI is no longer primarily a compute scaling problem.

It is becoming a data movement problem.

And data movement is limited by optical physics, not silicon performance.

Three structural points:

First, bandwidth demand in AI clusters is accelerating faster than electrical interconnect scaling.

Second, 1.6T and CPO architectures increase dependency on stable, high-quality laser sources.

Third, once optical components are designed into a platform, replacement cycles are long and sticky.

This changes how SIVE should be viewed.

Not as a cyclical supplier tied to component demand.

But as a potential design-in layer in optical reference architectures.

The bull case is not near-term revenue growth.

It is architectural adoption.

If Sivers becomes embedded in next-generation optical designs, demand becomes structural across hyperscaler buildouts.

Risks remain significant:

early-stage business model
historical dilution
customer concentration
execution risk in scaling photonics manufacturing

This is still a high-volatility setup.

But the key re-rating driver is simple:

Control points in AI systems are shifting from compute to connectivity, and from connectivity to optical physics.

That is where Sivers sits.@aleabitoreddit 
#StockMarket  #Investing

text:
$ALMU $ALMU continues building the team needed to move from R&D to commercial production.

The company appointed Brendan Moran, Ph.D. as Vice President of Engineering, a key hire as Aeluma prepares to scale manufacturing of its compound semiconductor technology.

Moran brings 18 years at Lumileds, where he led product strategy and development for a mobile flash business generating over $300 million in annual revenue. He also oversaw the launch of 25+ products into high-volume manufacturing, producing more than 4 billion semiconductor devices, and managed engineering teams responsible for taking products from development to mass production.

Why this matters:

Developing breakthrough semiconductor technology is only half the challenge. The harder part is turning lab-scale innovation into reliable, high-volume manufacturing that customers can deploy. That’s exactly where Moran’s experience fits.

For Aeluma, the focus is now shifting from proving its technology to commercializing it across AI infrastructure, optical communications, defense, aerospace, quantum computing, AR/VR, automotive and advanced sensing.

Aeluma’s platform integrates high-performance compound semiconductors with standard silicon manufacturing, aiming to deliver the performance advantages of III-V materials while using scalable semiconductor production processes. If successful, this could lower manufacturing costs while enabling broader adoption in fast-growing AI and photonics markets.

For investors, this is more than a management hire. It’s another signal that Aeluma is preparing for its next phase—moving beyond R&D and positioning itself for commercial revenue, manufacturing scale, and customer deployment.

text:
The physical AI trade feels intuitive because the demand narrative is so visible, but structurally it’s much harder than photonics or memory to anchor on a true bottleneck. 

In memory/optics you can point to very specific capacity and process constraints; with many of these AI hardware names, the value chain is still fluid and the eventual mass‑production suppliers aren’t locked in yet.

I think the better approach is to treat it as an ecosystem that will evolve toward a diversified supplier base, rather than trying to guess the one or two permanent choke points this early. 

The more interesting edge may end up being in companies whose economics improve as the chain diversifies, instead of in whoever looks like “the bottleneck” today.

I was scrolling trough my feed today, and the only thing I see is people comparing Quantum (2025) to photonics. 

Yeah we seen a very good run for quantum and then it just dumped. But I know why it dumped. Because THERE WAS NO REAL REVENUE. 

Comparing to photonics names like $AAOI they have real revenue and huge projections. So don’t compare some temporary bs to something real.

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI 

The recent hammering of photonics/CPO names, which was partly triggered by the SemiAnalysis note pushing timelines out for CPO adoption, looks like a short-sighted overreaction rather than a collapse in the fundamental thesis imo.

The realistic path is to use copper for scale-up as long as you can on short-reach, low-latency links inside the rack/cluster, and then move to optics as the final phase of the transition. The move to optics is fundamentally necessary. Copper interconnects eventually hit physical limits w/ exponentially rising power draw, signal degradation, and restricted reach as AI clusters scale to massive sizes w/ extreme bandwidth demands. Copper simply cannot efficiently move data across longer distances and at the densities required w/o consuming unsustainable power or compromising performance. 

This hybrid, phased reality is not a bear case. Copper staying relevant longer actually extends the runway and de-risks CPO adoption as clusters scale to a point where copper hits hard walls.

We’re still early in the multi-year AI infrastructure buildout. The “slow ramp” narrative today is exactly what you should be expect anyways before the volumes compound in the back half of the decade.

text:
Dear @vonderleyen,
How wonderful that you and the entire College of Commissioners travelled to Cork today to visit the Tyndall National Institute! A real “innovation powerhouse,” as you so aptly put it. “From test to scale, from ideas to industry” – that sounds like proper European future music. And Ireland really is a beautiful country. The green hills, the Guinness, the friendly people… a business trip like that must be quite enjoyable. The European taxpayer is surely delighted that you’re all so motivated and on the road.
Just one small, modest question out of pure fiscal curiosity: Did you know that right on your doorstep in Leuven, Belgium, there is an institute that is considered the global benchmark in exactly this field – integrated semiconductors, nanoelectronics, photonics and deep-tech hardware?
imec employs over 6,500 scientists, engineers and innovators from more than 100 countries. That’s more than ten times the size of Tyndall (which had a proud 580 staff and students in 2024, including 173 postgraduates with 158 PhD students). imec was Belgium’s top patent applicant in 2024/2025 and is driving major European Chips Act pilot lines. While Tyndall is getting an expansion worth over €100 million and works with around 200 industry partners, imec has had a global network for years and generates well over a billion euros in revenue annually – mostly through direct industry collaborations.
But of course: why stay in Leuven when you can fly the whole College, security teams, protocol staff and all the rest to Ireland instead? The taxpayers in Germany, France, Italy and all the other member states are happy to pay for a bit of extra European unity – plus flights, hotels, escorted convoys and the entire logistical effort for a visit to Cork, when Leuven is only about a half-hour drive from Brussels.
We really do think it’s nice that you praise Tyndall so much. The institute has been doing good work since it was founded in 2004 (with roots in the NMRC from 1981). But if you’re going to celebrate European top-tier research, then please do it where it’s biggest, most established and closest. It would save money – and CO₂. The Irish government can still be happy anyway – they’ve already received plenty of EU funds.
With friendly but slightly puzzled regards,

text:
The  Category Creator Portfolio
Some businesses aren’t just “leaders” — they’ve built moats so wide, nobody’s even trying to cross them.

$PLTR — Palantir Not a “software company.” It’s the only platform doing AI-driven ontology at classified government + enterprise scale simultaneously. Competitors sell tools. Palantir sells outcomes baked into defense and intelligence infrastructure that took a decade to build trust for.

$SPOT — Spotify Global audio distribution + the algorithm that actually knows what you want to hear next. Apple Music and YouTube Music are label-first. Spotify is user-first. That’s why churn stays low and podcasting/audiobooks keep expanding the moat.

$HIMS — Hims & Hers Direct-to-consumer telehealth with its own pharmacy, its own compounding, its own brand trust in categories people are embarrassed to walk into a clinic for. Nobody else owns the full stack front-to-back like this.

$TSLA — Tesla Not just EVs. Vertically integrated batteries, FSD data flywheel, Optimus robotics, and a charging network competitors now pay to use. The closest comps aren’t even in the same business anymore.

$AMD — Advanced Micro Devices The only credible alternative to Nvidia at scale in AI compute, while also owning x86 CPU share from Intel on the other side. Two-front dominance nobody else can claim.

$DUOL — Duolingo Language learning + gamification + AI tutoring in one free-to-paid funnel with network effects (leaderboards, streaks, social) that no edtech competitor has replicated at this user count.

$IBRX — ImmunityBio Proprietary IL-15 superagonist platform (Anktiva) — a completely different immunotherapy mechanism than PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. First-in-class, not best-in-class.

$ASTS — AST SpaceMobile Direct-to-cell satellite broadband from space, compatible with unmodified smartphones. Starlink’s version needs a partnered carrier workaround. AST built the actual satellites for it — patented spectrum + tech nobody else has cleared.

$RKLB — Rocket Lab Only company besides SpaceX with a proven, reusable, frequently-flying orbital rocket AND a full space systems manufacturing arm (satellites, components, Neutron coming). Everyone else is still pre-revenue or single-product.

$POET — POET Technologies Optical interposer platform integrating photonics at the chip level for AI datacenter interconnects. Nobody else has this exact manufacturing approach at this stage — first-mover on a bottleneck the whole AI buildout needs solved.

$OKLO — Oklo Fast fission microreactor design with NRC engagement and a direct line to hyperscaler power deals. Most nuclear names are still legacy large-reactor. Oklo’s the closest thing to “AI power, deployed.”

$CRWV — CoreWeave Purpose-built GPU cloud from day one, not a retrofit of general cloud infra. Hyperscaler-scale AI compute contracts without hyperscaler legacy overhead — a category it effectively created.

$MDA — MDA Space Only Western company with proven satellite bus manufacturing + robotics heritage (Canadarm lineage) at commercial scale — critical infrastructure nobody’s replicating fast.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 31}]}, "text": "The Category Creator Portfolio Some businesses aren’t just “leaders” — they’ve built moats so wide, nobody’s even trying to cross them. $PLTR — Palantir Not a “software company.” It’s the only platform doing AI-driven ontology at classified government + enterprise scale simultaneously. Competitors sell tools. Palantir sells outcomes baked into defense and intelligence infrastructure that took a decade to build trust for. $SPOT — Spotify Global audio distribution + the algorithm that actually knows what you want to hear next. Apple Music and YouTube Music are label-first. Spotify is user-first. That’s why churn stays low and podcasting/audiobooks keep expanding the moat. $HIMS — Hims & Hers Direct-to-consumer telehealth with its own pharmacy, its own compounding, its own brand trust in categories people are embarrassed to walk into a clinic for. Nobody else owns the full stack front-to-back like this. $TSLA — Tesla Not just EVs. Vertically integrated batteries, FSD data flywheel, Optimus robotics, and a charging network competitors now pay to use. The closest comps aren’t even in the same business anymore. $AMD — Advanced Micro Devices The only credible alternative to Nvidia at scale in AI compute, while also owning x86 CPU share from Intel on the other side. Two-front dominance nobody else can claim. $DUOL — Duolingo Language learning + gamification + AI tutoring in one free-to-paid funnel with network effects (leaderboards, streaks, social) that no edtech competitor has replicated at this user count. $IBRX — ImmunityBio Proprietary IL-15 superagonist platform (Anktiva) — a completely different immunotherapy mechanism than PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. First-in-class, not best-in-class. $ASTS — AST SpaceMobile Direct-to-cell satellite broadband from space, compatible with unmodified smartphones. Starlink’s version needs a partnered carrier workaround. AST built the actual satellites for it — patented spectrum + tech nobody else has cleared. $RKLB — Rocket Lab Only company besides SpaceX with a proven, reusable, frequently-flying orbital rocket AND a full space systems manufacturing arm (satellites, components, Neutron coming). Everyone else is still pre-revenue or single-product. $POET — POET Technologies Optical interposer platform integrating photonics at the chip level for AI datacenter interconnects. Nobody else has this exact manufacturing approach at this stage — first-mover on a bottleneck the whole AI buildout needs solved. $OKLO — Oklo Fast fission microreactor design with NRC engagement and a direct line to hyperscaler power deals. Most nuclear names are still legacy large-reactor. Oklo’s the closest thing to “AI power, deployed.” $CRWV — CoreWeave Purpose-built GPU cloud from day one, not a retrofit of general cloud infra. Hyperscaler-scale AI compute contracts without hyperscaler legacy overhead — a category it effectively created. $MDA — MDA Space Only Western company with proven satellite bus manufacturing + robotics heritage (Canadarm lineage) at commercial scale — critical infrastructure nobody’s replicating fast."}}}, "rest_id": "2073108049321984362", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5943", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
IT/Tech Highlights – July 3, 2026

Microsoft launched a $2-2.5 billion AI Unit employing ~6,000 forward-deployment engineers to help companies adopt AI and generate ROI. OpenAI proposed giving the Trump Administration a 5% stake in the company for potential public benefits from growth. Tata Electronics data breach under investigation by Indian authorities after leaking Apple iPhone 18 Pro supplier information. LG debuted its Gallery TV (canvas-style art TV) at CES 2026 previews, competing with Samsung’s The Frame.

Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra camera and flagship upgrades are leaking. iOS 27 public beta rollout is expected this month. Linux 6.19 kernel benchmarks show optimizations. Meta’s Zuckerberg noted AI agent tech is progressing slower than expected. Kuaishou’s Kling AI raised $2.8B (Alibaba/Tencent backing) at $15B pre-money valuation. NVIDIA/SK Hynix AI memory partnership and Google-SpaceX/xAI compute deal reports add momentum. Ongoing quantum, PQC, and hardware interconnect developments (CPO/photonics) continue.

#FiberForge #AITech #DataCenters

text:
$INDI might be one of the more interesting underfollowed semiconductor names in the Edge AI / robotics / perception space.

Everyone is chasing $AMBA after the Edge AI move, but indie Semiconductor is sitting in a very similar ecosystem: automotive semis, ADAS, radar, LiDAR, computer vision, ultrasonic sensing, photonics and perception software.

Rough setup: around ~$900M market cap, Q1 2026 revenue of $55.5M and Q2 guidance of $59M–$65M. So this is not some pre-revenue dream stock. It is a real semiconductor business with real customers, but still small enough where the upside could be meaningful if the story starts working.

The interesting part is that $INDI is not just a car chip story anymore. They are expanding into physical AI, humanoid robotics, AMRs, quantum and adjacent industrial applications. They also recently received a $25M radar chipset production order from a Tier 1 partner, driven by two automotive OEM customers.

That’s exactly the type of signal I want to see in a small-cap semi name: real revenue, real design wins, exposure to large megatrends and still not a mega-cap valuation.

The bull case is simple: cars, robots, drones, warehouses and smart machines all need better perception. They need radar, vision, LiDAR, software and low-power sensing at the edge. If physical AI and autonomous systems keep scaling, perception semiconductors could become one of the most important bottlenecks.

The bear case is obvious too: losses are still real, profitability is not here yet, automotive cycles can be brutal and execution has to improve.

But if the market starts looking past GPUs and realizes that AI also needs sensors, perception and edge processing in the real world, $INDI could become one of those names people suddenly rediscover.

Small cap. Real revenue. Edge AI exposure. Physical AI optionality.

Definitely on my watchlist.

NFA.

text: @JulienTechInvst Le plus marrant dans l’histoire c’est les mecs qui se plaignent que le sell-off sur les photonics s’est fait à la suite d’une note SA destiné aux instits alors que c’est ce qui se passe avec la recherche sell-side depuis 40 ans.. bref pas assez de diversification et trop de greed

The question is not whether light can compute.

The question is: why does it matter now?

The photonics ecosystem is reaching the point where larger and more sophisticated integrated circuits are becoming possible—but greater scale also makes physical errors, drift, loss, and fabrication variation harder to control.

QP2.0 was built to address that challenge.

#quantum #quantumcomputing #photonics

text:
“Putting out to Pasture”  – A cartoon that illustrates how silicon is soon reaching the end of its life for computing.

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure.

That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale.

The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti  and @PsiQuantum  are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.”

Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs  and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving.

The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon.

AI has already had its “ChatGPT moment,” moving almost overnight from research labs into everyday life and turning software into something that can talk, reason, write, code and assist. Yet the current AI boom still depends on a brute-force progress of building ever more chips, more power and ever-larger infrastructure. That explains why so much capital is now flowing into data-centre architecture, from bigger clusters to new power infrastructure. In the short term, this is necessary, because AI companies need capacity now and the market is rewarding whoever can deliver it fastest. But we are stretching silicon, electricity grids and classical computing architectures to do work they were never designed to handle at this scale. The more important shift is not simply toward larger server farms, but toward a post-silicon computing stack in which AI, quantum computing and biocomputing reinforce one another. Quantum computing is aimed at problems classical machines struggle with, including molecular simulation, materials discovery and cryptography. Companies such as @IBM , @IonQ_Inc , @rigetti and @PsiQuantum are attacking the problem through different hardware paths, from superconducting qubits to trapped ions and photonics. As Google Quantum AI founder Hartmut Neven said after the Willow milestone, “useful, very large quantum computers can indeed be built.” Biocomputing represents the other frontier, replacing the logic of forcing nature into silicon with the idea of using biology itself as a computational medium. Companies such as @CorticalLabs are developing neuron-based “wetware” systems, while @BiomemoryLabs and Catalog are working on DNA-based data storage for ultra-dense, long-term archiving. The future will not see AI replaced, but just harnessed differently. Where AI becomes the intelligence layer, quantum provides the simulation power for the hardest problems, and biocomputing offers density and efficiency. The future is not bigger data centres forever, but a stranger and smarter computing stack beyond silicon. #FutureOfComputing #AIInfrastructure #iantoons"}}}, "rest_id": "2073084679486316594", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "52", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Agentic AI is starting to do the work of a full scouting team, now in private markets. @earlysignalsvc  points it at private tech companies and scores them the week they raise, launch, or leave stealth, not the week they trend.

The whole edge in markets is acting while a signal is still fresh, before everyone else has read the same headline. Public equities are already a crowded place to try that, which is the game the DeepSeek Portfolio plays every week. Doing it in venture, at the moment a company raises or leaves stealth, is a much earlier point on the curve.

Forty-four companies ranked this week, each on six scored dimensions plus a potential and a scoop score. Photonics for AI data centers, infrastructure for long-running agents, on-device writing tools. Systematic scoring pushed all the way to the front of the funnel.

Good work. Worth a look.

text:
This post from relatively unknown account @0xBADB01E is going viral within the AI community - here's why

The core claim is genuinely contrarian and flips how we think about scaling architectures and supply chain constraints: 

> premise: the wires between chips matter more than the chips themselves or the memory attached to them

> if you designed an AI inference machine starting from the interconnect and working backwards, you'd build something radically different (and cheaper) than an Nvidia GPU

> GPUs are terrible at generating tokens - when an LLM generates text one token at a time (decode), a Blackwell GPU uses under 20% of its math capability as the compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from memory

IOW, you bought a Ferrari engine and it spends 80% of its time waiting at the gas pump

> the intuitive answer is "add faster memory" (more HBM) but the smarter answer is: since a single chip can't have infinite memory bandwidth, split the model across many chips and let each chip handle a slice

> the problem then shifts because the constraint is no longer memory bandwidth, it's how fast chips can talk to each other (the interconnect)

> Nvidia's interconnect is the wrong approach - NVLink is a firehose with huge bandwidth, but high latency (delay per message)

> that was a deliberate choice for training, but not ideal for inference where the data crossing between chips per token is absurdly tiny 

It's like paying a $50 shipping-and-handling fee on a $1 product

The contrarian take is that everyone says "decode is memory-bandwidth bound," but once you shard the model, per-user speed is gated by how quickly the tiny inter-chip messages complete, not by how fat the memory pipe is - decode is latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound

> if latency is what matters, you can build around slow, cheap memory (LPDDR instead of HBM), because its access latency is about the same as HBM's; only its bandwidth is worse

> then you only need enough FLOPs to match that memory

The above two statements have big implications:

1. this is a direct thesis against HBM's centrality in the compute buildout

2. the packaging supply chain that's currently the binding constraint on Nvidia's output matters much less

3. for photonics, it can potentially go either way - depends on if optical is focused on improving bandwidth (bearish) or latency (bullish)

I don't know this account but this post was interesting and intelligent so I'm here for the conversation that follows

As an example,", "id_str": "2073990357398982797", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "0xBADB01E", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "2073970411138949533", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "1811787044722479105", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 52, "reply_count": 136, "retweet_count": 187, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1811787044722479105"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzM5OTAzNTcxNzI1NTE2ODA=", "text": "First you have to understand that modern LLM inference already disaggregates weights as models outgrew single chips years ago. You shard either by layer (pipeline parallelism) or by slicing every layer (tensor parallelism), and the two do very different things. As an example, let’s look at Llama 3.3. It has 70B of weights and at FP8 that’s 70 GB of memory which is enough to fit on a single H100. Now that H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM, so the fastest it can ever decode for one user is 70/3.35 ≈ 21 ms/token or ~48 tok/s while using under 1% of its FLOPs. Now if we pipeline it across 8 chips: each chip holds ~8.75 GB, which means it only needs 1/8th the bandwidth and 1/8th the FLOPs to sustain the same aggregate throughput. Now crucially the token/sec a user gets is limited by the amount of data that crosses the link. In current LLMs all that is a small amount of activations for LLama 3.3 it’s ~8 KB per token…. Yes, you read that right it’s 8 KILOBYTES we are sending over a <900 GB/s link. That’s only 9 ns of serialization time but the overhead of 224G PAM4 SerDes adds ~100 ns per link traversal with RS-FEC which is 11x longer than the payload itself. And then you have the NVSwitch adding ~300 ns per hop and you need to pay twice. That’s ~600 ns of just hardware latency wrapped around 9 ns of data making a 98% tax before software even shows up. Then NCCL’s collective stack turns 600 ns into 10-20+ us… all to move 8 kilobytes lol. For comparison 8 KB serializes over 10 Gigabit Ethernet NRZ, in just 6.6 us. Pipeline parallelism however doesn’t make a single user faster as the token still needs to visit every layer in the sequence, so per-user speed is still weights / per-chip bandwidth. To get more speed per user token you need to use tensor parallelism and have all the chips work on the same layer simultaneously. TP costs you 2 all reduce OPs per layer, 160 per token on llama 3, that’s still kilobytes of traffic but with NVLink overhead it’s a massive tax and why pipeline parallelism on most models still gives more interactivity per user. However, this gives you a huge latency lever to pull that scales tokens per second with interconnect speed instead of memory BW. The clever amongst you might have also realized that sharding doesn’t just cut memory bandwidth per chip it also cuts FLOPs per chip and is why we have such bad MFU on decode. So once you’ve sized the link for the memory, you need to size the compute for it too. This is called “balancing the pipeline”, and currently no shipping chip does it because they were all designed as standalone monsters. Remember Tokens/sec = ~aggregate memory BW / bytes touched per token. At batch 64 in FP4 you need ~250 FLOPs per byte, and Blackwell ships 1,250. Provisioned 5x more than the narrow pipe of HBM. Nobody saturates shit cause they are all building around HBM. So now it all comes full circle. Parallelism reduces memory bw pressure and thus FLOPs but increases interconnect latency pressure. Despite having HBM and GigaSERDES we aren’t actually doing more work lol. But if you really wanted to balance the pipeline you need to match the memory bandwidth, the flops, and most importantly the interconnect. So what does that look like ? Well if you build around LPDDR’s lower bandwidth, lower your interconnect latency, you actually can beat Nvidia on decode with a fraction of the silicon."}}}, "rest_id": "2073990357398982797", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1429103", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074201112006242337", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4521", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$BURU @NUBURUlasers is extending its laser platform from counter-UAS to underwater autonomy.

Through Lyocon Italian laser-tech factory and photonics center of excellence, NUBURU is working with Suncubes to connect blue-laser technology, beam-control and optical power transmission into new Defense & Security applications.

✅Recent PRs highlight two strategic directions:
- Laser Arm: vehicle-integrated directed-energy and counter-UAS systems.
- DEEP LIGHT: blue-laser power-beaming for underwater autonomy, subsea power and maritime infrastructure.

The opportunity is not only one project. It is the potential validation of NUBURU blue-laser technology in mission-critical environments where power, endurance and optical performance matter.
Lyocon provides the laser engine. 
☀️SunCubes provides beam-control and optical power capabilities. NUBURU builds the platform.

text:
amazing!

"MIT researchers developed 2 new photonic coupling technologies, the Evanescent Coupler and GRIN Coupler, that could enable future chip packages to exceed 1 petabit per second (1,000 Tb/s) of communication bandwidth."

"Instead of sending data through power hungry copper wires, the system uses light (photonics) to move information between chips, dramatically increasing bandwidth while reducing energy consumption and heat."

"Researchers designed the technology to be compatible with existing CMOS semiconductor manufacturing, potentially making ultra-high bandwidth photonic chip packages practical for future AI accelerators, supercomputers and hyperscale data centers."

text:
There's a company almost no one is talking about, trading near NAV, that sells into three of the hottest markets right now: AI chipmaking, defense batteries, and photonics.

And it has several unfair advantages:
→ $100M Department of Energy grant.
→ Samsung SDI building and backing its battery factory.
→ A 2028 law that bans the US military from buying Chinese battery cells.

That company is @ForgeNano, and the vehicle taking it public at a $1.2B valuation is Archimedes Tech SPAC Partners II $ATII, run by the same team that took SoundHound $SOUN public in 2022.

Forge Nano sells atomic-scale coatings, protective layers thousands of times thinner than a human hair, that make batteries last longer and chips fail less.

$ATII trades at $10.88/share today against a trust worth ~$10.63/share, so the downside from here is ~2%. And you can buy $ATIIW if you want the leveraged upside via warrants.

I've done deep dives (and bought) into SPAC setups like this before:
→ Boost Run $BRUN pre-merger (originally $WLAC).
→ Exascale Labs $BCAR, still pre-merger.

My full 6k-word deep dive on $ATII will be released tomorrow. Link in bio to read it when it comes out!

Thanks to @SPAC_zilla and @stepnotonpets for the idea!

text:
The silicon photonics supply chain diagram is one of the most important investment maps in technology right now (Save this).

Every AI data center being built depends on what's in that chart and the companies inside it are just starting to show what the revenue ramp looks like.

Here are the 10 stocks I am watching.

1. Broadcom (AVGO): Broadcom's AI revenue rocketed 106% to $8.4 billion in its most recent quarter, driven by custom accelerator chips for Google, Meta, and Anthropic, plus AI networking silicon. It is one of the few companies doing both the switch silicon and the photonics integration needed for co-packaged optics at hyperscale. The silicon photonics diagram shows Broadcom appearing across multiple layers, PIC design, laser sources, photodiode, and EIC design.

2. MACOM Technology (MTSI): MACOM is quietly positioning itself at the inflection point of the 1.6T and 3.2T optical transceiver transition. In March 2026 it launched 448G PAM4 modulator drivers among the first in the industry and joined Broadcom, Cisco, and Semtech in the 400G Optical MSA standards consortium. In June 2026 it introduced hot via chip scale technology, eliminating wire bonds in its AlGaAs packaging.

3. Marvell Technology (MRVL): Marvell is the DSP engine inside many of the optical modules in that chart. Its custom AI silicon, built for hyperscalers who want to own their accelerator architecture is the fastest growing part of its business. It sits at the EIC design layer and supplies the digital signal processing that makes high-speed optical links work reliably at scale. As the industry shifts from 400G to 800G and 1.6T, every step requires more sophisticated DSPs and Marvell captures more revenue per module shipped.

4. Keysight Technologies (KEYS): Every optical module that ships from every fab in that chart has to be tested before it reaches a data center. Keysight is the global leader in electronic and photonic test equipment. As optical speeds push to 800G and beyond, the testing instrumentation has to keep pace and it commands premium pricing.

5. FormFactor (FORM): FormFactor is the most underappreciated name in this entire chart. Q1 2026 revenue was $226 million, up 32% year over year, beating estimates with non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 versus $0.44 expected. It acquired Keystone Photonics in December 2025, becoming the leading wafer-level silicon photonics test platform for co-packaged optics production. Its partnership with Advantest created the world's fastest automated photonic alignment test system with nine-axis nano-precision. Over 100 of the world's leading silicon photonics manufacturers use FormFactor systems meaning every CPO chip that ships from any fab in that diagram gets tested on FormFactor equipment.

6. Teradyne (TER): Teradyne sits in the E/O Testing layer alongside Keysight and FormFactor. As silicon photonics chips get more complex, integrating lasers, modulators, photodetectors and electronic drivers on a single chip,  the test complexity explodes. Teradyne's automated test equipment platforms are expanding from traditional semiconductor testing into photonic integrated circuit validation. It also has a robotics division, which ties the silicon photonics thesis directly back to the humanoid supply chain story.

7. EXFO (EXFO): EXFO is a fiber optic and network testing specialist that has been building testing platforms specifically for coherent optical and silicon photonics applications. It sits directly in the E/O Testing box of the supply chain map. It's a smaller cap, which means the upside from the optical buildout is amplified relative to its size. It is one of the few companies that tests live network optical performance meaning it gets pulled in not just at the component manufacturing stage but every time a data center expands or upgrades.

8. Foxconn Industrial Internet (FXCOF): Foxconn appears twice in that supply chain diagram in both Photonics Assembly and Optical Interconnect and that is before you even consider its server business. Q1 2026 revenue hit $66.6 billion, up 29.7% year over year, with AI servers now representing more than 50% of total server revenue. Its silicon photonics CPO switches entered mass production in Q3 2026 with full year shipments forecast at 10,000 units. It is boosting capex 30% specifically for AI infrastructure. Almost no retail investors think of Foxconn as a silicon photonics play and that's the opportunity.

9. Sumitomo Electric (SMTOY): Sumitomo appears in two places on that supply chain diagram as an optical interconnect component supplier and in the laser/photodiode layer. The company has been a foundational supplier to the fiber optic industry for decades and is now scaling its silicon photonics packaging and optical connectivity capabilities for AI data center applications.

10. Synopsys (SNPS): Every silicon photonics chip has to be designed before it can be manufactured. Synopsys is the dominant EDA software company for photonic integrated circuit design and sits at the very top left of that supply chain map. The shift toward co-packaged optics means optical and electronic chip design have to be co-simulated, exactly the kind of complex, multi physics workflow Synopsys is built for. Its photonics design tools are already embedded across every major chip company in that diagram. The more complex the chip, the more customers pay for Synopsys tools.

Milk Road Pro is tracking every layer of the silicon photonics supply chain, from Broadcom and Marvell to the testing names most investors still don’t know.

Join Milk Road Pro for the full breakdown and for all our AI trades for just $1 using  the link below!

text:
$SIVE is up ~1,700% in 2026, and following every tracked call on it returned +234% YTD on schwerelos.

Sivers makes the laser arrays for co-packaged optics, the interconnect AI data centers need to move data at 1.6 terabits. GlobalFoundries just signed them into its silicon-photonics platform, aimed at the $25B pluggable-optics market.

3 scored calls tracked, 1 closed in profit.

text:
Nomura report : You're not deep enough in the AI Semiconductor stack yet. You're fishing with the crowd. 

Buying GPU, CPU or foundry are a crowded trade now. Nomura released the map to the depths. Here's the rundown: You'll surely want to bookmark this

Investment implications run much deeper than GPU demand. For decades, chip performance gains came from one lever: shrink the transistor. At 3nm and below, that lever is breaking. 

> New growth model: three pillars: 3D transistors (GAA evolving to stacked cFET), backside power delivery (separating power and signal networks, reducing IR drop), and materials substitution. The capex ramp starts 2026. Mass production hits 2027.

> Photolithography: High-NA EUV tools cost $400M each and require metal-oxide photoresists that run $10,000–$40,000/gallon. Current EUV resists cost $5,000/gallon. That's a 2–8x unit price step-up in a material most investors have never heard of. This is before high-NA even hits mass production (2029).

> Advanced packaging: SoIC hybrid bonding replaces microbumps with direct Cu-to-Cu connections, taking AI chip bandwidth from 200 GB/s to 1 TB/s. It requires nanometer-level wafer flatness, meaning CMP steps increase from 45–55 to 55–70 per chip (20–30% jump). BESI is positioned as the dominant equipment supplier here.

> Glass substrates: Glass-core substrates replace ABF organic substrates for large, high-power AI chips. Lower CTE, better flatness, lower signal loss. Broadcom adopts first for switch ASICs in 2027. Intel follows. The bottleneck is TGV (Through-Glass Via) formation - laser drilling, etching, metal fill, planarization. Companies with proprietary TGV process IP (LPKF, E&E) are entering this supply chain now.

> Photonics:  Indium phosphide supply stays tight 2025–2027 due to export controls and yield constraints. The alternative is photonic SOI wafers: 25% the cost of InP, scalable for CPO. Soitec owns ~70% of global photonic SOI share. Demand enters rapid growth by 2027.

> Silicon wafers: Three technology waves (backside power delivery, wafer-bonded NAND, photonic SOI) stack onto baseline demand growth of 5%, adding 4–6 percentage points annually. Total 12-inch silicon wafer demand approaches 10% growth p.a. Supply cannot keep pace. Gap opens in 2027 Pricing power returns to GlobalWafers, Shin-Etsu, SUMCO.

> TSMC the amplifier: $70B capex in 2027 alone. 26 advanced fabs globally. Increasing localized procurement across lithography consumables, CMP, specialty gases, wafers, and packaging materials. Regional suppliers certified now see disproportionate order flow.

Which layer of this supply chain do you think is most mispriced right now - equipment, materials, substrates, or optical?

2026 is ramp setup, 2027 is the inflection, 2028–2030 is full adoption. Best investment can be made by knowing which player is essential for which phase. 

Repost this. Most people following the AI trade are one layer too shallow. Your followers will thank you.

You're fishing with the crowd. Buying GPU, CPU or foundry are a crowded trade now. Nomura released the map to the depths. Here's the rundown: You'll surely want to bookmark this Investment implications run much deeper than GPU demand. For decades, chip performance gains came from one lever: shrink the transistor. At 3nm and below, that lever is breaking. > New growth model: three pillars: 3D transistors (GAA evolving to stacked cFET), backside power delivery (separating power and signal networks, reducing IR drop), and materials substitution. The capex ramp starts 2026. Mass production hits 2027. > Photolithography: High-NA EUV tools cost $400M each and require metal-oxide photoresists that run $10,000–$40,000/gallon. Current EUV resists cost $5,000/gallon. That's a 2–8x unit price step-up in a material most investors have never heard of. This is before high-NA even hits mass production (2029). > Advanced packaging: SoIC hybrid bonding replaces microbumps with direct Cu-to-Cu connections, taking AI chip bandwidth from 200 GB/s to 1 TB/s. It requires nanometer-level wafer flatness, meaning CMP steps increase from 45–55 to 55–70 per chip (20–30% jump). BESI is positioned as the dominant equipment supplier here. > Glass substrates: Glass-core substrates replace ABF organic substrates for large, high-power AI chips. Lower CTE, better flatness, lower signal loss. Broadcom adopts first for switch ASICs in 2027. Intel follows. The bottleneck is TGV (Through-Glass Via) formation - laser drilling, etching, metal fill, planarization. Companies with proprietary TGV process IP (LPKF, E&E) are entering this supply chain now. > Photonics: Indium phosphide supply stays tight 2025–2027 due to export controls and yield constraints. The alternative is photonic SOI wafers: 25% the cost of InP, scalable for CPO. Soitec owns ~70% of global photonic SOI share. Demand enters rapid growth by 2027. > Silicon wafers: Three technology waves (backside power delivery, wafer-bonded NAND, photonic SOI) stack onto baseline demand growth of 5%, adding 4–6 percentage points annually. Total 12-inch silicon wafer demand approaches 10% growth p.a. Supply cannot keep pace. Gap opens in 2027 Pricing power returns to GlobalWafers, Shin-Etsu, SUMCO. > TSMC the amplifier: $70B capex in 2027 alone. 26 advanced fabs globally. Increasing localized procurement across lithography consumables, CMP, specialty gases, wafers, and packaging materials. Regional suppliers certified now see disproportionate order flow. Which layer of this supply chain do you think is most mispriced right now - equipment, materials, substrates, or optical? 2026 is ramp setup, 2027 is the inflection, 2028–2030 is full adoption. Best investment can be made by knowing which player is essential for which phase. Repost this. Most people following the AI trade are one layer too shallow. We are in $SIVE $SIVEF since $4.9, and still holding strong. Photonics in general have dumped heavily but these pullbacks are needed as for the next leg up.

Looking to add more if it goes near our entry again

Sivers operates as a fab-lite model so this is likely hinting at foundry allocation/scaling for CW DFB laser ramp across Jabil, and their other partners. It could also point to broader foundry allocation/scaling beyond the already announced WIN partnership. My feedback is that it's very bullish if it's comprised of long-only institutions and there's no heavy discount (30 day VWAP). Otherwise, would need to reaccess if there's arb or short term investors. This is the perfect time to get ideal strategic investors like $GFS on the cap table. text:
$SIVE is raising ~$61M (600M SEK) to expand manufacturing capacity for InP lasers and optical amplifiers.

Sivers operates as a fab-lite model so this is likely hinting at foundry allocation/scaling for CW DFB laser ramp across Jabil, and their other partners.  

It could also point to broader foundry allocation/scaling beyond the already announced WIN partnership. 

My feedback is that it's very bullish if it's comprised of long-only institutions and there's no heavy discount (30 day VWAP). Otherwise, would need to reaccess if there's arb or short term investors. 

This is the perfect time to get ideal strategic investors like $GFS on the cap table. As well as focusing on having more US institutional support. 

Especially considering $56M is a small check to a single US institution that want photonics exposure (eg. look at Poet's $400M registered direct offering for sentiment). 

A lot of it is "it depends when more info comes out", but this looks like a very positive signal for mass production given Siver's fab-lite models.

text:
As AI, robotics, autonomous vehicles, data centers, and grid electrification continue to drive electricity demand, I believe energy will be one of the defining investment themes of this decade.

I just published a post highlighting the exact key levels where I believe these 16 energy stocks offer the most attractive buying opportunities.

Subscribers can check it out and use the levels as a reference while planning their entries.

Next, I’ll be dropping the key institutional buy levels for photonics, robotics, and neocloud stocks. Stay tuned.

text:
QP2.0 applies a composite-pulse approach to integrated photonics, with the goal of improving operational fidelity and robustness against fabrication variability.

The platform is designed for applications across quantum computing, quantum routing, and classical light processing.

Its core benefits include higher fidelity, lower loss, better fabrication tolerance, and greater robustness to variation.

#IntegratedPhotonics #QuantumComputing #Photonics

text:
I can see why the physical AI trade looks so attractive. But I think a multiple of the investment ideas here will fail compared to photonics or memory. The market is structurally very different, with far fewer, or even no, clear bottlenecks.

On top of that, we’re still so early that it’s not even clear who will actually end up being a supplier for mass production. I wouldn’t spend too much time trying to hunt for the bottleneck here. 

The industry has enough time to build a broadly diversified supplier base.
